The problem is that `Email.__init__` does not execute `CommaSeparatedEmailField.__init__`. So the following unit test never runs CommaSeparateEmailField's processing and therefore fails:

```python
 def test_models_repr(self):
        self.assertEqual(repr(Email(to='test@example.com')), "<Email: ['test@example.com']>")
```

The output of repr() is simply `<Email: 'test@example.com'>`. The preprocessing/validation must be done in a form instead and `Email.__init__` simply expects the one and correct `['test@example.com']` list value from its caller.

The only way to make that unit test pass is to either override `Email.__init__` (which Django does not recommend) or to use signals as I did above. Alternatively, maybe it's best to consider the test as broken because it tests something that Django doesn't do. 

Consider the following example:

```python
from django.db.models import Model, IntegerField
class MyModel(Model):
    number = IntegerField()
    def __repr__(self):
        return "MyModel(number=%s)" % repr(self.number)

my_instance = MyModel(number="obviously_not_an_integer")
print(repr(my_instance))
```

Which will simply output `MyModel(number='obviously_not_an_integer')` even though my_instance.number clearly conflicts with the IntegerField for the same reason.

I wrote it this way to cover the case where there is not enough chain history to provide an accurate average block time.

With your formula, the avg time would get set to `block_1_timestamp - genesis_timestamp` if queried on the 1st block on the chain.  Then it wouldn't get refreshed until `cache_expiration_seconds` later.

The current code would result in it getting updated at approximately blocks 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, ... until the chain history was at least `cache_expiration_seconds` long after which if would update at regular intervals.

Thoughts on whether the complexity is worth covering this admittedly minority use case?

Eh, the link I posted is of mostly academic interest. The sampling procedure boils down to generating `num_sessions` draws from a random distribution and then normalizing to obtain a random list of `num_sessions` probabilities that sum to 1.

The Dirichlet connection comes in that you can view `lines_in_each_session` as a multinomial random variable that we could sample e.g. using https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multinomial_distribution#Sampling_from_a_multinomial_distribution. But we don't really care about `lines_in_each_session` actually having a multinomial distribution. Sampling a Dirichlet is a convenient way of giving us something similar, literally choosing a random multinomial distribution (represented by its parameters).

Maybe the real question is to what degree the test even relies on `lines_in_each_session` being random....

No worries here, but a point of note when using percent (`%`) formatting in Python. To be extra super-duper safe, you should always pass a tuple:

```python
>>> format = 'see %s'
>>> value = 'this'
>>> format % value
'see this'
>>> value = ('a', 'real', 'tuple', 9)
>>> format % value
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: not all arguments converted during string formatting
>>> format % (value,)
"see ('a', 'real', 'tuple', 9)"
```

In this case it's obvious we aren't accidentally passing a tuple as the single argument

```

'662253be-c'
```

Why don't we use all alnum chars to shorten the string representation:

```
>>> base64.b64encode(os.urandom(9),'-_')
'j7Sm6BO_7-pr'
```
